definition: GDP is the total dollar value of everything a country produces — every good made, every service sold — within a given period. Think of it as the economy's annual report card.
context: When GDP grows, the economy is expanding — more jobs, rising incomes, stronger corporate profits. When it shrinks for two quarters in a row, that's the textbook definition of a recession.
formula: GDP = Consumer Spending + Business Investment + Government Spending + (Exports − Imports)
insight: Q4 2025 stumbled to 1.4% — dragged by the federal government shutdown and tariff headwinds. The slowdown is expected to be temporary; economists project a bounce-back in early 2026 as those one-off drags fade and consumer spending stays resilient.
source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis · Feb 2026

definition: Prices go up over time — that's inflation. CPI measures exactly how much. It tracks a basket of everyday items — groceries, rent, gas, clothing — and shows how much more (or less) they cost compared to a year ago. When CPI climbs fast, your money buys less than it used to.
context: The Fed watches CPI closely alongside PCE. When CPI runs persistently above 2%, the Fed considers raising rates to cool demand. When it falls sharply, it may signal a weakening economy.
formula: Tracks price changes across 8 categories: Food & Beverage, Housing, Apparel, Transportation, Medical Care, Recreation, Education, and Other.
insight: Shelter costs keep headline CPI sticky above target — rent and equivalent homeowner costs make up ~35% of the index and are slow to fall. The Fed is watching core CPI closely; until it drops toward 2.5%, rate cuts remain on hold.
source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics · Feb 2026

definition: The single most powerful interest rate in the world. It's what banks charge each other for overnight loans — and it ripples through everything else: your mortgage rate, your car loan, your credit card APR. When the Fed raises it, borrowing gets more expensive across the board.
context: The FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee) meets 8 times per year to review the rate. Every quarter-point move ripples through mortgages, car loans, credit cards, and bond yields within days.
formula: When FFR rises → borrowing costs increase → spending slows → inflation cools. When FFR falls → cheaper credit → spending rises → economy stimulated.
insight: The Fed is in wait-and-see mode after three cuts in late 2025. Markets have 1–2 more cuts priced in for 2026, but only if inflation cooperates. Higher rates mean bond yields stay elevated — a challenge for growth stocks but an opportunity for fixed-income investors.

definition: Every first Friday of the month, the government reveals how many jobs were added or lost — excluding farm workers. It's the most market-moving number of the month. A big miss can send stocks down hundreds of points within minutes of the release.
context: Markets treat NFP as the most market-moving data release of the month. A strong print can delay rate cuts; a weak one can accelerate them. It's the first hard look at the prior month's economic activity.
formula: NFP > 150K/month → healthy labor market. NFP < 75K/month → potential slowdown. NFP negative → jobs being cut.
insight: Payrolls are cooling from 2025's strong pace but still positive — a soft landing signal. Wage growth above 4% keeps consumer spending firm, which supports corporate earnings but also keeps the Fed cautious about cutting rates too fast.
source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics · Feb 2026

definition: Similar to CPI, but the Fed trusts it more. PCE measures what Americans actually spend on goods and services — and it adjusts when people swap expensive items for cheaper ones. That makes it a more honest picture of real-world inflation. When PCE runs hot, rate cuts get pushed back.
context: When Fed officials say 'inflation is near our target,' they're talking about PCE, not CPI. Core PCE (excluding food and energy) is the number that drives rate decisions.
formula: PCE differs from CPI: it adjusts for substitution behavior — when prices rise, consumers switch to cheaper alternatives — making it a more realistic measure of actual purchasing patterns.
insight: Core PCE above target keeps the Fed cautious, but the pace of rise matters most — it's ticking up slowly, not surging. If it stalls or reverses in coming months, the door opens for mid-2026 rate cuts, which would benefit bond holders and rate-sensitive sectors like real estate.
source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis · Jan 2026

definition: A snapshot of how much interest the government pays to borrow money at different time horizons — 2 years, 10 years, 30 years. Normally, longer loans pay higher rates. When that flips — short-term rates above long-term ones — it's called an inversion, and every U.S. recession in the past 50 years was preceded by one.
context: Every U.S. recession since 1955 was preceded by a yield curve inversion. The curve inverted in mid-2022 and remained inverted for over two years — one of the longest inversions on record.
formula: Normal: 10Y yield > 2Y yield → growth expected. Inverted: 10Y yield < 2Y yield → recession signal. Uninverting: spread returning to positive → mixed signal.
insight: The curve is finally uninverting after a record inversion — historically a mixed signal. A sustained positive spread with improving growth data points to recovery; a quick re-inversion would be a warning sign.

definition: PMI surveys purchasing managers at hundreds of companies, asking whether activity — new orders, production, employment, deliveries — is expanding, contracting, or unchanged versus the prior month.
context: PMI is released on the first business day of each month — making it one of the earliest reads on economic momentum. Because it's a survey, it reflects expectations and intentions, not just lagging results.
formula: Above 50 → sector is expandingBelow 50 → sector is contractingTwo separate indexes: Manufacturing PMI and Services PMI
insight: Manufacturing dipped back below 50 — a contraction signal — but services at 53.5 are keeping the broader economy afloat. Since services dominate the U.S. economy (~80% of GDP), a strong services PMI matters more for overall growth than a weak factory reading.

definition: PPI measures the average change in prices that domestic producers receive for their output — raw materials, intermediate goods, and finished products. It's CPI's upstream cousin: what businesses pay before consumers do.
context: PPI is considered a leading indicator for consumer inflation. When input costs rise for producers, those costs typically get passed downstream to consumers within 1–3 months.
formula: PPI rising → production costs climbing → CPI likely to followPPI falling → deflationary pressure building upstream
insight: PPI running above CPI signals that producers are absorbing some cost increases rather than fully passing them on — that margin compression often shows up in earnings misses. If producers start passing costs through, CPI could re-accelerate in coming months.

definition: The VIX measures the market's expectation of S&P 500 volatility over the next 30 days, derived from the prices of S&P 500 options. It is widely called the "fear gauge" of Wall Street.
context: VIX and the S&P 500 tend to move inversely — when stocks fall, VIX spikes. Savvy investors watch VIX for timing: extreme VIX spikes often mark capitulation lows, while extremely low VIX can signal complacency.
formula: VIX < 15 → calm, complacent marketVIX 15–25 → moderate uncertaintyVIX > 30 → elevated fear / market stressVIX > 40 → crisis-level panic (seen in 2008, 2020)
insight: VIX above 20 signals elevated market anxiety — not panic, but meaningful near-term uncertainty. For long-term ETF holders, elevated VIX can be an opportunity to add to positions at better prices, as vol spikes often overshoot and revert. Avoid reactionary selling.

definition: DXY measures the value of the U.S. dollar against a basket of six major currencies — primarily the Euro (57.6%), Japanese Yen (13.6%), British Pound (11.9%), and three others. A higher DXY means a stronger dollar.
context: DXY is the global benchmark for dollar strength. It influences commodities (most priced in USD), emerging markets (dollar-denominated debt becomes harder to service), and multinational corporate earnings.
formula: Strong dollar → U.S. imports cheaper · exports more expensive · foreign earnings worth less in USDWeak dollar → exports competitive · international ETFs get a currency boost
insight: A weakening dollar is a tailwind for international ETFs (EFA, VEA, EEM) — foreign gains translate into more USD when you convert back. It also boosts commodity prices and eases pressure on emerging-market dollar-denominated debt. Watch for dollar stabilization if the Fed pauses cuts.

definition: JOLTS counts how many job openings exist across the U.S. economy at month-end, plus how many workers were hired, quit, or laid off. It paints a picture of labor market dynamics, not just the headline count.
context: Fed Chair Powell watches JOLTS closely. When workers quit readily, it means they're confident they can find better jobs — a sign of a hot labor market that could sustain wage inflation.
formula: Key ratio: Job Openings ÷ Unemployed WorkersRatio > 1.0 → more jobs than people looking → tight marketThe "Quits Rate" — voluntary resignations — is the Fed's favorite confidence gauge
insight: The quits rate at 2.0% — down from a 2.4% peak — signals workers feel less confident about jumping to new jobs. This reduces wage growth pressure, which is exactly what the Fed wants to see before cutting rates. A soft landing means openings cool gradually, not collapse.

definition: Initial jobless claims counts the number of Americans who filed for unemployment benefits for the first time in a given week. It's the most timely labor market indicator available — data is just one week old.
context: Released every Thursday morning, claims move markets immediately. The 4-week moving average smooths out holiday noise and weather distortions — it's the number economists focus on, not the weekly print.
formula: < 250K/week → healthy labor market250–350K → softening, watch closely> 350K sustained → recession-level job losses
insight: Claims near 220K remain historically low — the labor market isn't cracking. As long as claims stay below 250K, the economy is generating more jobs than it's losing. Watch for sustained moves above 260–280K as an early warning signal worth acting on.

definition: Retail Sales measures total receipts at stores that sell consumer goods — from auto dealers to online retailers to gas stations. It is the most direct monthly read on consumer spending, which drives ~70% of U.S. GDP.
context: Because consumer spending is the economy's engine, retail sales moves can shift Fed expectations instantly. A sustained softening signals demand destruction; a rebound signals resilience.
formula: Reported month-over-month (MoM) — a positive print means consumers spent more"Control group" (ex-auto, gas, building materials) is what flows directly into GDP calculations
insight: December's −0.9% was weather and post-holiday payback; January's modest rebound signals consumers are still spending — just cautiously. The ex-auto control group at +0.8% is the cleanest signal of underlying demand. Consumer resilience supports earnings for discretionary and retail ETFs.

definition: Housing Starts counts the number of new residential construction projects begun in a given month, reported as an annualized rate. It measures the pulse of homebuilding activity across the U.S.
context: Housing is one of the most interest-rate-sensitive sectors in the economy. When rates rise, starts fall; when rates fall, starts recover. Watching housing starts tells you whether rate policy is actually filtering through to the real economy.
formula: Starts > 1.5M annualized → healthy construction paceBuilding Permits (released same day) lead Starts by 1–2 monthsHighly sensitive to mortgage rates — the rate-cut barometer
insight: Housing is stuck — constrained by 6.9% mortgage rates that keep both builders and buyers cautious. A meaningful recovery needs the 10Y Treasury to fall toward 3.5–4.0%. When housing starts recover, it will confirm that Fed rate cuts are working — a bullish signal for homebuilder and REIT ETFs.

definition: A credit spread is the extra yield investors demand on corporate bonds above equivalent U.S. Treasury yields. It represents the price of default risk — how much extra return the market requires to lend to companies instead of the government.
context: Credit spreads widen before recessions as investors price in rising default risk. They are a leading indicator of financial stress — often signaling trouble before it shows up in GDP or unemployment data.
full_name: High Yield & Investment Grade Bond Spreads
formula: Tight spreads (<300 bps HY) → market calm, credit conditions easyWide spreads (>500 bps HY) → stress, tightening financial conditionsIG spreads typically run 80–150 bps; HY spreads 250–600 bps in normal cycles
insight: Spreads widening to 380 bps signals rising corporate stress — but still below the ~500 bps level that historically precedes recessions. For ETF investors, widening HY spreads are a reason to reduce exposure to junk bond funds (HYG, JNK) and favor investment-grade or Treasury ETFs until the trend reverses.

definition: The real interest rate strips out inflation from the nominal rate, showing what borrowers and savers actually earn or pay in purchasing-power terms. It tells you whether monetary policy is truly restrictive or just nominally high.
context: During 2022–23, the Fed raised the FFR sharply while inflation ran above it — keeping the real rate negative and policy looser than it appeared. Only when the FFR exceeded inflation did policy become truly restrictive. The neutral real rate is estimated at ~0.5%.
full_name: Real Interest Rate (FFR minus Core PCE)
formula: Real Rate = Nominal Rate − Inflation (Core PCE)Positive real rate → policy is genuinely restrictiveNegative real rate → policy is stimulative (money is "cheap")
insight: A real rate of +1.58% — well above the neutral ~0.5% — means policy is genuinely restrictive: borrowing is expensive in real terms, suppressing investment and spending. As inflation falls or the Fed cuts, the real rate will ease — that's the moment to expect a broader risk-asset rally and recovery in rate-sensitive sectors.

definition: The Conference Board surveys ~3,000 households monthly, asking how they feel about current business conditions, the job market, and their expectations six months ahead. It produces an index benchmarked to 100 (1985 baseline).
context: Consumer confidence drives spending, which drives ~70% of GDP. When it falls sharply, watch retail and consumer discretionary ETFs. The gap between Present Situation and Expectations is often more revealing than the headline number.
formula: Two components:Present Situation Index — how things feel right nowExpectations Index — outlook for jobs and income in 6 monthsWhen Present > Expectations: consumers confident today but worried about tomorrow
insight: The Present–Expectations gap is wide: consumers feel fine today but are anxious about the next 6 months. This divergence often precedes a pullback in discretionary spending. Watch consumer staples ETFs (XLP) as a defensive tilt when confidence drops sharply.

definition: The U of Michigan surveys ~500 households twice a month, measuring how Americans feel about their personal finances, buying conditions, and economic outlook. Released in preliminary and final form, it is the Fed's preferred sentiment gauge.
context: Unlike the Conference Board's measure, Michigan captures inflation psychology directly. When consumers expect high inflation, they demand higher wages and spend faster — creating the very inflation they fear. The Fed watches this feedback loop carefully.
full_name: University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index
formula: Index range: 0–100 (1964 baseline = 100)Includes a crucial sub-measure: 1-year and 5-year inflation expectationsFed watches these expectations closely — if they become "unanchored," the Fed must act
insight: Michigan sentiment has fallen sharply from above 80 a year ago — consumers are genuinely worried about future inflation and earnings resilience. The 1-year inflation expectation at 2.8% is inching up, which is why the Fed remains cautious about cutting rates aggressively. Watch for further deterioration in the "expectations" component as a recession warning.

definition: Industrial Production measures the real output of manufacturing, mining, and utilities sectors — the "physical economy" that makes goods. Released monthly by the Federal Reserve, it's one of the four indicators the NBER uses to date recessions.
context: As the U.S. economy shifted to services, IP covers roughly 20% of GDP — but it's a leading indicator of business investment and hiring cycles in goods-producing industries, which punch above their weight in economic cycles.
formula: Paired with Capacity Utilization: how much of the economy's factory capacity is in useUtilization > 80% → inflationary pressure (economy running hot)Utilization < 77% → slack exists, no inflation pressure
insight: The January dip follows December's utilities surge — not a trend reversal. Capacity at 77.1% shows slack in the industrial economy, which is disinflationary — good for inflation expectations but suggests limited upside for industrial and materials ETFs (XLI, XLB) until demand accelerates.

definition: Durable Goods Orders measures new orders placed with U.S. manufacturers for items expected to last three or more years — aircraft, machinery, computers, autos, appliances. It's a forward-looking gauge of business investment intentions.
context: The "core capital goods" sub-component is what economists watch most closely — it captures whether businesses are ordering new equipment and machinery, a forward signal for productivity and growth.
formula: Headline: total durable goods orders (volatile — dominated by aircraft)Core: ex-transportation = steadier readCore Capital Goods (ex-defense, ex-aircraft) = best proxy for business investment plans
chart_label: Durable Goods Orders % Change MoM — Recent
insight: The +3.1% headline surge was almost entirely Boeing aircraft orders — highly volatile, not repeatable. Strip that out and the picture is flat. Businesses aren't ramping capital investment yet, which limits upside for industrials ETFs but also means the economy isn't overheating.

definition: The breakeven inflation rate is what the bond market expects average annual inflation to be over the next 10 years. It's derived from the yield difference between regular Treasury bonds and TIPS (Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities).
context: Unlike CPI or PCE, which measure inflation that has already happened, breakeven inflation shows what the market expects. It's a real-time signal watched by every fixed-income investor and Fed official daily.
formula: Breakeven Rate = 10Y Nominal Treasury Yield − 10Y TIPS Real YieldIf breakeven > Fed's 2% target → market doubts the Fed can hit its targetIf breakeven < 2% → market expects below-target inflation
insight: With breakevens rising above 2.3%, bond markets are signaling that inflation will stay above target for the next decade. This reduces the case for rate cuts and keeps pressure on long-duration bonds (TLT). TIPS themselves become attractive when breakevens are rising — they pay out more as inflation rises.

definition: Import prices track how much U.S. businesses pay for goods purchased from abroad — everything from foreign electronics and clothing to oil and pharmaceuticals. Rising import prices mean inflation is being "imported" into the domestic economy.
context: With sweeping new tariffs in effect, import prices have become one of the most watched inflation gauges of 2025–26. The BLS releases both import and export price indexes together monthly.
formula: Import Price Index → feeds into PPI within ~1 month → feeds into CPI within ~2–3 monthsKey driver: tariffs are import price increases by definition — a 10% tariff = 10% import price shock on affected goods
insight: Rising import prices — partly tariff-driven — feed into producer and consumer prices within months. This is a second-wave inflation risk: even if demand is cooling, cost-push inflation from tariffs can keep CPI elevated regardless. Companies with heavy import exposure (retail, consumer goods) face margin pressure.

definition: The BEA's monthly Personal Income and Outlays report tracks how much Americans earned and spent. It's released on the same day as PCE — in fact, PCE inflation is derived from this same data. Together they tell the full consumer story.
context: When income growth outpaces spending, consumers are building a cushion — a healthy dynamic. When spending outpaces income, it signals either credit-fueled consumption or savings depletion, both of which are unsustainable.
formula: Personal Income growth > Spending growth → savings rate rises → disinflationarySpending growth > Income growth → consumers drawing down savings → unsustainablePersonal Savings Rate = (Income − Spending) / Income
insight: Income growing faster than spending is a disinflationary signal — consumers are saving, not splurging. The savings rate tick-up to 4.6% shows resilience, not distress. This dynamic supports a soft landing and allows the Fed room to cut without reigniting demand-driven inflation.

definition: U-6 is the broadest official measure of unemployment. It counts not just the jobless (U-3 headline rate), but also discouraged workers who've stopped looking and people working part-time who want full-time work. It reveals labor market slack hidden in the headline.
context: When the U-6/U-3 gap widens, it means more people are working below their capacity — a sign of a cooling labor market even when the headline looks healthy. The Fed watches this to gauge true wage pressure potential.
formula: U-3 (Headline): Unemployed ÷ Labor ForceU-6 (Broad): (Unemployed + Marginally Attached + Part-Time for Economic Reasons) ÷ Labor ForceGap between U-6 and U-3 = degree of underemployment in the economy
insight: U-6 rising to 8.0% while U-3 holds at 4.1% means the gap is widening — more workers are stuck in part-time or marginally attached situations. This signals more labor market slack than the headline implies, which is actually good for inflation control but bad for consumer spending power and wage growth momentum.

definition: LFPR is the percentage of the civilian non-institutional population (age 16+) that is either employed or actively looking for work. It measures how much of America's potential workforce is actually in the labor market.
context: The U.S. LFPR peaked at 67.3% in early 2000, dropped post-2008, and fell further during COVID. Its recovery is a long-term structural story — aging demographics make a full return to pre-2000 levels unlikely.
formula: LFPR = Labor Force ÷ Civilian Non-Institutional Population × 100Declining LFPR → workers leaving workforce → unemployment rate falls "artificially"Prime-Age LFPR (25–54 year olds) = cleanest signal, less affected by aging demographics
insight: The overall LFPR drag is mostly demographic — retiring boomers, not discouraged workers. Prime-age LFPR at 83.7% is nearly fully recovered. This distinction matters: if prime-age workers are participating, the labor supply is healthy; the headline decline isn't a crisis signal but a structural demographic reality.

definition: Four times a year, each of the 18 FOMC members submits their anonymous projection for where the Federal Funds Rate should be at year-end. Plotted as dots on a chart, this "dot plot" gives markets a direct window into Fed thinking on the rate path.
context: Markets move sharply when the median dot shifts. A "hawkish dot plot" (fewer cuts than expected) sends bond yields up; a "dovish" one (more cuts) rallies bonds and growth stocks. It's released quarterly alongside the Fed's economic forecasts.
formula: Median dot = official Fed guidance on rate pathDispersion of dots = degree of disagreement within the FedWide dispersion → high uncertainty; tight cluster → strong consensus
chart_label: FOMC Member Projections for 2026 Year-End Rate (18 members)
insight: 10 of 18 members expect only 0–1 cuts in 2026 — more hawkish than markets hoped. The wide dispersion signals genuine uncertainty. Every new inflation or employment print reshuffles the dots. For investors, this means rate-sensitive positions (real estate, utilities, long bonds) face headwinds until the picture clarifies.

definition: Quantitative Tightening is the Fed's process of shrinking its balance sheet by allowing Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities (MBS) to mature without reinvesting the proceeds. It is the opposite of QE (Quantitative Easing), which expanded the balance sheet by purchasing these assets.
context: QT puts upward pressure on long-term Treasury yields by reducing a major buyer from the market. It's a form of tightening that works independently from rate hikes, making the total policy stance tighter than the FFR alone suggests.
full_name: Quantitative Tightening / Fed Balance Sheet Runoff
formula: QE: Fed buys bonds → adds money to system → lower yields, more creditQT: Fed lets bonds mature → removes money from system → upward pressure on yieldsBalance sheet peaked at $9.0T (Apr 2022); now ~$6.8T — reduced by ~$2.2T
insight: QT at $25B/month means less Fed buying of Treasuries — the government must find private buyers for more of its debt. This keeps long-term yields elevated independent of the FFR. When the Fed eventually pauses QT (expected when reserves approach a "comfortable" floor), long bonds could rally significantly.

definition: M2 measures the total supply of money in the U.S. economy — physical cash, checking accounts, savings accounts, money market funds, and small CDs. It is the broadest widely-used measure of "how much money is sloshing around."
context: M2 surged 27% in 2020–21 (COVID stimulus) and then contracted in 2022–23 as the Fed tightened. Its recent re-acceleration to ~4% growth is being watched closely as a potential leading indicator of renewed inflation pressure.
formula: M1: Cash + checking accounts (most liquid)M2: M1 + savings + money market funds + small CDsMilton Friedman: "Inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon" — M2 growth predicts inflation with a 12–18 month lag
chart_label: M2 Money Supply YoY Growth Rate — Monthly
insight: M2 growing at 4% after shrinking in 2023 means monetary conditions are easing again. Historically, sustained M2 growth above 5–6% precedes inflation with a 12–18 month lag. This is why monetarist economists are cautious about declaring victory on inflation — money supply says the fight isn't fully won.

definition: Every quarter, the Fed surveys ~80 large U.S. banks and ~24 foreign banks on their lending standards. The key question: are you tightening or easing credit conditions for business loans, mortgages, and consumer credit? The net percentage is the signal.
context: When banks tighten, businesses struggle to borrow and invest. SLOOS has predicted every recession since the 1990s — a sustained move above +30% net tightening is a reliable red flag. Fed Chair Powell references SLOOS in almost every press conference.
full_name: Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey on Bank Lending Practices
formula: Net % Tightening = % of banks tightening − % of banks easingPositive number → more banks making credit harder to getNegative number → more banks loosening credit (stimulative)SLOOS leads economic activity by ~6–9 months
chart_label: Net % of Banks Tightening C&I Loan Standards — Quarterly
insight: Still net tightening, but the trend is clearly improving — a positive leading signal. When SLOOS returns to net neutral or easing, it typically precedes a credit expansion cycle 6–9 months later, which benefits small-cap stocks (more bank-dependent) and high-yield bonds. Watch for Q1 2026 survey results in April.

definition: CAPE divides the S&P 500 price by the average of the past 10 years of real (inflation-adjusted) earnings. Developed by Nobel laureate Robert Shiller, it smooths out business cycle volatility to give a cleaner picture of whether stocks are cheap or expensive historically.
context: CAPE is not a market timing tool — it has been "elevated" since the 1990s without predicting exact peak dates. But its long-run predictive power for 10-year returns is well documented: high CAPE → below-average returns over the next decade.
formula: CAPE = S&P 500 Price ÷ 10-Year Average Real EarningsHistorical average CAPE: ~17CAPE > 30 → historically expensive; low expected 10-year forward returnsCAPE < 15 → historically cheap; high expected forward returns
insight: At 34.8 — roughly double the historical average — U.S. equities are priced for perfection. Historical data suggests below-average 10-year returns from this level. This doesn't mean sell everything, but it does argue for diversification: international ETFs (VEA, EEM) trade at far lower CAPE ratios and offer better long-term value.

definition: Gold is the oldest financial asset and the world's ultimate store of value and safe-haven currency. Its price reflects a combination of inflation expectations, real interest rates, geopolitical stress, central bank demand, and the strength of the U.S. dollar.
context: Central banks — especially China, India, Poland, and Turkey — have been buying gold at record pace since 2022 as a hedge against U.S. dollar dominance. This structural demand has suppressed gold's typical inverse relationship with rising real rates.
formula: Gold rises when: real rates fall · dollar weakens · inflation fears rise · geopolitical risk spikes · central banks buyGold falls when: real rates rise · dollar strengthens · risk appetite returnsGold has no yield — its opportunity cost is the real interest rate
insight: Gold at all-time highs alongside elevated real rates (which typically suppress gold) is unusual — driven by central bank de-dollarization buying and geopolitical hedging. A 5–10% gold allocation via GLD or IAU provides portfolio diversification; gold's correlation to equities has been near zero over the past decade.

definition: WTI is the benchmark price for U.S.-produced crude oil, set at trading hubs in Cushing, Oklahoma. It is one of the most widely traded commodities in the world — and because oil touches almost every sector of the economy, its price ripples through inflation, corporate margins, and consumer spending.
context: Oil price is set by global supply/demand, OPEC+ production decisions, U.S. shale production, geopolitical events, and the strength of the dollar (oil is priced in USD globally). It is one of the most "noisy" indicators but also one of the fastest to move.
full_name: WTI Crude Oil Price (West Texas Intermediate)
formula: Oil high → gasoline prices rise · transportation costs rise · CPI risesOil low → consumer "tax cut" effect · airline, shipping margins improve · but signals weak demandEvery $10/bbl rise in oil adds approximately 0.2–0.3% to U.S. CPI
insight: Oil near $70 is a Goldilocks level — low enough not to spike CPI, high enough not to signal demand collapse. OPEC+ production cuts are preventing a price drop that would reflect weak global growth. Watch for moves above $85 (inflation risk) or below $60 (demand recession signal) as the levels that would change the macro story.

definition: EPS growth measures how much corporate earnings increased (or fell) compared to the same quarter a year ago. For the S&P 500, it represents the aggregate earnings power of America's 500 largest public companies — the foundation of stock market valuations.
context: Every quarter, ~500 companies report results over a 4-week earnings season. "Beats rate" (% that exceed consensus estimates) and forward guidance matter as much as the actual numbers — stocks are priced on future expectations, not past results.
formula: Stock prices = EPS × P/E multipleEarnings drive long-run returns; multiple expansion drives short-run movesEPS misses → stocks fall · EPS beats → stocks rally — especially during earnings season
insight: Eight percent earnings growth despite macro headwinds is solid. Markets are pricing in +11.3% for 2026 — which requires continued economic resilience. If macro softens or tariff costs bite into margins, earnings estimate cuts would likely compress the S&P 500's rich valuation. Watch guidance language in Q1 2026 earnings reports.

definition: The trade balance measures the difference between U.S. exports and imports. When imports exceed exports (the U.S. norm), the result is a trade deficit. The U.S. has run a persistent trade deficit for decades — it is the world's largest goods importer.
context: The trade deficit is politically charged but economically nuanced. A widening deficit can actually signal a strong economy (consumers buying imported goods). However, tariffs aimed at reducing the deficit often trigger retaliatory actions that hurt U.S. exporters.
full_name: U.S. International Trade in Goods & Services
formula: Trade Balance = Exports − ImportsNegative = trade deficit (U.S. buys more from the world than it sells)Widening deficit → GDP math drag (net exports subtract from GDP)But reflects strong domestic demand — not necessarily a sign of weakness
insight: The record January deficit reflects companies front-loading imports ahead of tariffs — pulling demand forward. This will mechanically drag Q1 2026 GDP growth lower, but it's a distortion, not a signal of fundamental weakness. Expect the deficit to narrow once the pre-tariff rush subsides, which could provide a GDP bounce in Q2.

definition: The effective tariff rate is the average tax the U.S. government collects on all imported goods — calculated as total tariff revenue divided by total import value. It reflects the aggregate impact of all tariff schedules across all trading partners.
context: The U.S. effective tariff rate averaged ~2.5% for two decades before 2025. The sweeping tariff actions of 2025–26 have pushed it toward levels last seen in the 1930s (Smoot-Hawley era), representing the most significant trade policy shift in nearly a century.
full_name: U.S. Effective Tariff Rate on Imports
formula: Effective Tariff Rate = Tariff Revenue Collected ÷ Total Import ValueA 10% blanket tariff doesn't raise the effective rate by 10% immediately — some imports are exempt, others face retaliatory tariffs that reduce import volumePass-through to consumers: typically 80–100% within 6–12 months
chart_label: U.S. Effective Tariff Rate — Policy Timeline
insight: Tariffs at these levels are effectively a consumption tax — paid by U.S. importers and passed to consumers. For ETF investors, this means pressure on import-heavy retail and consumer goods companies, and a potential boost for domestic manufacturers. Watch earnings guidance for explicit tariff cost commentary starting in Q1 2026 reports.

definition: USD/CNY measures how many Chinese yuan one U.S. dollar buys. A higher number means a weaker yuan (dollar stronger). China's central bank (PBOC) sets a daily "fixing" rate and allows the yuan to trade within a ±2% band around it — making this a managed, not freely floating, exchange rate.
context: The yuan is the global barometer of U.S.–China trade tensions. When tariffs escalate, China often allows controlled yuan depreciation to offset competitiveness losses. Rapid depreciation beyond 7.50 historically triggers EM currency contagion and global risk-off moves.
formula: Stronger yuan (lower USD/CNY) → Chinese exports more expensive · easier to import · PBOC looseningWeaker yuan (higher USD/CNY) → Chinese exports cheaper · offsets some tariff impact · capital outflow riskOffshore CNH rate (freely traded in Hong Kong) often diverges from onshore CNY
insight: Yuan at 7.25 is elevated but within managed territory — the PBOC fix at 7.22 signals they're not allowing rapid depreciation. A controlled yuan limits tariff offset for China but preserves financial stability. If USD/CNY breaks 7.50, watch for a broader EM currency sell-off affecting EM ETFs (EEM, VWO) globally.
